functions.name: route
functions.docstring:
Decorate a view function to register it with the given URL
rule and options. Calls :meth:`add_url_rule`, which has more
details about the implementation.

.. code-block:: python

    @app.route("/")
    def index():
        return "Hello, World!"

See :ref:`url-route-registrations`.

The endpoint name for the route defaults to the name of the view
function if the ``endpoint`` parameter isn't passed.

The ``methods`` parameter defaults to ``["GET"]``. ``HEAD`` and
``OPTIONS`` are added automatically.

:param rule: The URL rule string.
:param options: Extra options passed to the
    :class:`~werkzeug.routing.Rule` object.
functions.code:
def route(self, rule: str, **options: t.Any) -> t.Callable[[T_route], T_route]:
        """Decorate a view function to register it with the given URL
        rule and options. Calls :meth:`add_url_rule`, which has more
        details about the implementation.

        .. code-block:: python

            @app.route("/")
            def index():
                return "Hello, World!"

        See :ref:`url-route-registrations`.

        The endpoint name for the route defaults to the name of the view
        function if the ``endpoint`` parameter isn't passed.

        The ``methods`` parameter defaults to ``["GET"]``. ``HEAD`` and
        ``OPTIONS`` are added automatically.

        :param rule: The URL rule string.
        :param options: Extra options passed to the
            :class:`~werkzeug.routing.Rule` object.
        """

        def decorator(f: T_route) -> T_route:
            endpoint = options.pop("endpoint", None)
            self.add_url_rule(rule, endpoint, f, **options)
            return f

        return decorator

functions.name: add_url_rule
functions.docstring:
Register a rule for routing incoming requests and building
URLs. The :meth:`route` decorator is a shortcut to call this
with the ``view_func`` argument. These are equivalent:

.. code-block:: python

    @app.route("/")
    def index():
        ...

.. code-block:: python

    def index():
        ...

    app.add_url_rule("/", view_func=index)

See :ref:`url-route-registrations`.

The endpoint name for the route defaults to the name of the view
function if the ``endpoint`` parameter isn't passed. An error
will be raised if a function has already been registered for the
endpoint.

The ``methods`` parameter defaults to ``["GET"]``. ``HEAD`` is
always added automatically, and ``OPTIONS`` is added
automatically by default.

``view_func`` does not necessarily need to be passed, but if the
rule should participate in routing an endpoint name must be
associated with a view function at some point with the
:meth:`endpoint` decorator.

.. code-block:: python

    app.add_url_rule("/", endpoint="index")

    @app.endpoint("index")
    def index():
        ...

If ``view_func`` has a ``required_methods`` attribute, those
methods are added to the passed and automatic methods. If it
has a ``provide_automatic_methods`` attribute, it is used as the
default if the parameter is not passed.

:param rule: The URL rule string.
:param endpoint: The endpoint name to associate with the rule
    and view function. Used when routing and building URLs.
    Defaults to ``view_func.__name__``.
:param view_func: The view function to associate with the
    endpoint name.
:param provide_automatic_options: Add the ``OPTIONS`` method and
    respond to ``OPTIONS`` requests automatically.
:param options: Extra options passed to the
    :class:`~werkzeug.routing.Rule` object.
functions.code:
def add_url_rule(
        self,
        rule: str,
        endpoint: str | None = None,
        view_func: ft.RouteCallable | None = None,
        provide_automatic_options: bool | None = None,
        **options: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        """Register a rule for routing incoming requests and building
        URLs. The :meth:`route` decorator is a shortcut to call this
        with the ``view_func`` argument. These are equivalent:

        .. code-block:: python

            @app.route("/")
            def index():
                ...

        .. code-block:: python

            def index():
                ...

            app.add_url_rule("/", view_func=index)

        See :ref:`url-route-registrations`.

        The endpoint name for the route defaults to the name of the view
        function if the ``endpoint`` parameter isn't passed. An error
        will be raised if a function has already been registered for the
        endpoint.

        The ``methods`` parameter defaults to ``["GET"]``. ``HEAD`` is
        always added automatically, and ``OPTIONS`` is added
        automatically by default.

        ``view_func`` does not necessarily need to be passed, but if the
        rule should participate in routing an endpoint name must be
        associated with a view function at some point with the
        :meth:`endpoint` decorator.

        .. code-block:: python

            app.add_url_rule("/", endpoint="index")

            @app.endpoint("index")
            def index():
                ...

        If ``view_func`` has a ``required_methods`` attribute, those
        methods are added to the passed and automatic methods. If it
        has a ``provide_automatic_methods`` attribute, it is used as the
        default if the parameter is not passed.

        :param rule: The URL rule string.
        :param endpoint: The endpoint name to associate with the rule
            and view function. Used when routing and building URLs.
            Defaults to ``view_func.__name__``.
        :param view_func: The view function to associate with the
            endpoint name.
        :param provide_automatic_options: Add the ``OPTIONS`` method and
            respond to ``OPTIONS`` requests automatically.
        :param options: Extra options passed to the
            :class:`~werkzeug.routing.Rule` object.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError

functions.name: endpoint
functions.docstring:
Decorate a view function to register it for the given
endpoint. Used if a rule is added without a ``view_func`` with
:meth:`add_url_rule`.

.. code-block:: python

    app.add_url_rule("/ex", endpoint="example")

    @app.endpoint("example")
    def example():
        ...

:param endpoint: The endpoint name to associate with the view
    function.
functions.code:
def endpoint(self, endpoint: str) -> t.Callable[[F], F]:
        """Decorate a view function to register it for the given
        endpoint. Used if a rule is added without a ``view_func`` with
        :meth:`add_url_rule`.

        .. code-block:: python

            app.add_url_rule("/ex", endpoint="example")

            @app.endpoint("example")
            def example():
                ...

        :param endpoint: The endpoint name to associate with the view
            function.
        """

        def decorator(f: F) -> F:
            self.view_functions[endpoint] = f
            return f

        return decorator

functions.name: before_request
functions.docstring:
Register a function to run before each request.

For example, this can be used to open a database connection, or
to load the logged in user from the session.

.. code-block:: python

    @app.before_request
    def load_user():
        if "user_id" in session:
            g.user = db.session.get(session["user_id"])

The function will be called without any arguments. If it returns
a non-``None`` value, the value is handled as if it was the
return value from the view, and further request handling is
stopped.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
executes before every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes before
every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
execute before every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.before_app_request`.
functions.code:
def before_request(self, f: T_before_request) -> T_before_request:
        """Register a function to run before each request.

        For example, this can be used to open a database connection, or
        to load the logged in user from the session.

        .. code-block:: python

            @app.before_request
            def load_user():
                if "user_id" in session:
                    g.user = db.session.get(session["user_id"])

        The function will be called without any arguments. If it returns
        a non-``None`` value, the value is handled as if it was the
        return value from the view, and further request handling is
        stopped.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        executes before every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes before
        every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
        execute before every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.before_app_request`.
        """
        self.before_request_funcs.setdefault(None, []).append(f)
        return f

functions.name: after_request
functions.docstring:
Register a function to run after each request to this object.

The function is called with the response object, and must return
a response object. This allows the functions to modify or
replace the response before it is sent.

If a function raises an exception, any remaining
``after_request`` functions will not be called. Therefore, this
should not be used for actions that must execute, such as to
close resources. Use :meth:`teardown_request` for that.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
executes after every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes after
every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
execute after every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.after_app_request`.
functions.code:
def after_request(self, f: T_after_request) -> T_after_request:
        """Register a function to run after each request to this object.

        The function is called with the response object, and must return
        a response object. This allows the functions to modify or
        replace the response before it is sent.

        If a function raises an exception, any remaining
        ``after_request`` functions will not be called. Therefore, this
        should not be used for actions that must execute, such as to
        close resources. Use :meth:`teardown_request` for that.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        executes after every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes after
        every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
        execute after every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.after_app_request`.
        """
        self.after_request_funcs.setdefault(None, []).append(f)
        return f

functions.name: teardown_request
functions.docstring:
Register a function to be called when the request context is
popped. Typically, this happens at the end of each request, but
contexts may be pushed manually during testing.

.. code-block:: python

    with app.test_request_context():
        ...

When the ``with`` block exits (or ``ctx.pop()`` is called), the
teardown functions are called just before the request context is
made inactive.

When a teardown function was called because of an unhandled
exception it will be passed an error object. If an
:meth:`errorhandler` is registered, it will handle the exception
and the teardown will not receive it.

Teardown functions must avoid raising exceptions. If they
execute code that might fail they must surround that code with a
``try``/``except`` block and log any errors.

The return values of teardown functions are ignored.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
executes after every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes after
every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
execute after every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.teardown_app_request`.
functions.code:
def teardown_request(self, f: T_teardown) -> T_teardown:
        """Register a function to be called when the request context is
        popped. Typically, this happens at the end of each request, but
        contexts may be pushed manually during testing.

        .. code-block:: python

            with app.test_request_context():
                ...

        When the ``with`` block exits (or ``ctx.pop()`` is called), the
        teardown functions are called just before the request context is
        made inactive.

        When a teardown function was called because of an unhandled
        exception it will be passed an error object. If an
        :meth:`errorhandler` is registered, it will handle the exception
        and the teardown will not receive it.

        Teardown functions must avoid raising exceptions. If they
        execute code that might fail they must surround that code with a
        ``try``/``except`` block and log any errors.

        The return values of teardown functions are ignored.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        executes after every request. When used on a blueprint, this executes after
        every request that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and
        execute after every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.teardown_app_request`.
        """
        self.teardown_request_funcs.setdefault(None, []).append(f)
        return f

functions.name: context_processor
functions.docstring:
Registers a template context processor function. These functions run before
rendering a template. The keys of the returned dict are added as variables
available in the template.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
is called for every rendered template. When used on a blueprint, this is called
for templates rendered from the blueprint's views. To register with a blueprint
and affect every template, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_context_processor`.
functions.code:
def context_processor(
        self,
        f: T_template_context_processor,
    ) -> T_template_context_processor:
        """Registers a template context processor function. These functions run before
        rendering a template. The keys of the returned dict are added as variables
        available in the template.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        is called for every rendered template. When used on a blueprint, this is called
        for templates rendered from the blueprint's views. To register with a blueprint
        and affect every template, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_context_processor`.
        """
        self.template_context_processors[None].append(f)
        return f

functions.name: url_value_preprocessor
functions.docstring:
Register a URL value preprocessor function for all view
functions in the application. These functions will be called before the
:meth:`before_request` functions.

The function can modify the values captured from the matched url before
they are passed to the view. For example, this can be used to pop a
common language code value and place it in ``g`` rather than pass it to
every view.

The function is passed the endpoint name and values dict. The return
value is ignored.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
is called for every request. When used on a blueprint, this is called for
requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and affect
every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_url_value_preprocessor`.
functions.code:
def url_value_preprocessor(
        self,
        f: T_url_value_preprocessor,
    ) -> T_url_value_preprocessor:
        """Register a URL value preprocessor function for all view
        functions in the application. These functions will be called before the
        :meth:`before_request` functions.

        The function can modify the values captured from the matched url before
        they are passed to the view. For example, this can be used to pop a
        common language code value and place it in ``g`` rather than pass it to
        every view.

        The function is passed the endpoint name and values dict. The return
        value is ignored.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        is called for every request. When used on a blueprint, this is called for
        requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and affect
        every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_url_value_preprocessor`.
        """
        self.url_value_preprocessors[None].append(f)
        return f

functions.name: url_defaults
functions.docstring:
Callback function for URL defaults for all view functions of the
application.  It's called with the endpoint and values and should
update the values passed in place.

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
is called for every request. When used on a blueprint, this is called for
requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and affect
every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_url_defaults`.
functions.code:
def url_defaults(self, f: T_url_defaults) -> T_url_defaults:
        """Callback function for URL defaults for all view functions of the
        application.  It's called with the endpoint and values and should
        update the values passed in place.

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        is called for every request. When used on a blueprint, this is called for
        requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint and affect
        every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_url_defaults`.
        """
        self.url_default_functions[None].append(f)
        return f

functions.name: errorhandler
functions.docstring:
Register a function to handle errors by code or exception class.

A decorator that is used to register a function given an
error code.  Example::

    @app.errorhandler(404)
    def page_not_found(error):
        return 'This page does not exist', 404

You can also register handlers for arbitrary exceptions::

    @app.errorhandler(DatabaseError)
    def special_exception_handler(error):
        return 'Database connection failed', 500

This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
can handle errors from every request. When used on a blueprint, this can handle
errors from requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint
and affect every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_errorhandler`.

.. versionadded:: 0.7
    Use :meth:`register_error_handler` instead of modifying
    :attr:`error_handler_spec` directly, for application wide error
    handlers.

.. versionadded:: 0.7
   One can now additionally also register custom exception types
   that do not necessarily have to be a subclass of the
   :class:`~werkzeug.exceptions.HTTPException` class.

:param code_or_exception: the code as integer for the handler, or
                          an arbitrary exception
functions.code:
def errorhandler(
        self, code_or_exception: type[Exception] | int
    ) -> t.Callable[[T_error_handler], T_error_handler]:
        """Register a function to handle errors by code or exception class.

        A decorator that is used to register a function given an
        error code.  Example::

            @app.errorhandler(404)
            def page_not_found(error):
                return 'This page does not exist', 404

        You can also register handlers for arbitrary exceptions::

            @app.errorhandler(DatabaseError)
            def special_exception_handler(error):
                return 'Database connection failed', 500

        This is available on both app and blueprint objects. When used on an app, this
        can handle errors from every request. When used on a blueprint, this can handle
        errors from requests that the blueprint handles. To register with a blueprint
        and affect every request, use :meth:`.Blueprint.app_errorhandler`.

        .. versionadded:: 0.7
            Use :meth:`register_error_handler` instead of modifying
            :attr:`error_handler_spec` directly, for application wide error
            handlers.

        .. versionadded:: 0.7
           One can now additionally also register custom exception types
           that do not necessarily have to be a subclass of the
           :class:`~werkzeug.exceptions.HTTPException` class.

        :param code_or_exception: the code as integer for the handler, or
                                  an arbitrary exception
        """

        def decorator(f: T_error_handler) -> T_error_handler:
            self.register_error_handler(code_or_exception, f)
            return f

        return decorator

functions.name: register_error_handler
functions.docstring:
Alternative error attach function to the :meth:`errorhandler`
decorator that is more straightforward to use for non decorator
usage.

.. versionadded:: 0.7
functions.code:
def register_error_handler(
        self,
        code_or_exception: type[Exception] | int,
        f: ft.ErrorHandlerCallable,
    ) -> None:
        """Alternative error attach function to the :meth:`errorhandler`
        decorator that is more straightforward to use for non decorator
        usage.

        .. versionadded:: 0.7
        """
        exc_class, code = self._get_exc_class_and_code(code_or_exception)
        self.error_handler_spec[None][code][exc_class] = f

functions.name: _get_exc_class_and_code
functions.docstring:
Get the exception class being handled. For HTTP status codes
or ``HTTPException`` subclasses, return both the exception and
status code.

:param exc_class_or_code: Any exception class, or an HTTP status
    code as an integer.

make_null_session
functions.docstring:
Creates a null session which acts as a replacement object if the
real session support could not be loaded due to a configuration
error.  This mainly aids the user experience because the job of the
null session is to still support lookup without complaining but
modifications are answered with a helpful error message of what
failed.

This creates an instance of :attr:`null_session_class` by default.
functions.code:
def make_null_session(self, app: Flask) -> NullSession:
        """Creates a null session which acts as a replacement object if the
        real session support could not be loaded due to a configuration
        error.  This mainly aids the user experience because the job of the
        null session is to still support lookup without complaining but
        modifications are answered with a helpful error message of what
        failed.

        This creates an instance of :attr:`null_session_class` by default.
        """
        return self.null_session_class()

functions.name: is_null_session
functions.docstring:
Checks if a given object is a null session.  Null sessions are
not asked to be saved.

This checks if the object is an instance of :attr:`null_session_class`
by default.
functions.code:
def is_null_session(self, obj: object) -> bool:
        """Checks if a given object is a null session.  Null sessions are
        not asked to be saved.

        This checks if the object is an instance of :attr:`null_session_class`
        by default.
        """
        return isinstance(obj, self.null_session_class)

functions.name: get_cookie_name
functions.docstring: The name of the session cookie. Uses``app.config["SESSION_COOKIE_NAME"]``.

functions.name: get_cookie_domain
functions.docstring:
The value of the ``Domain`` parameter on the session cookie. If not set,
browsers will only send the cookie to the exact domain it was set from.
Otherwise, they will send it to any subdomain of the given value as well.

Uses the :data:`SESSION_COOKIE_DOMAIN` config.

.. versionchanged:: 2.3
    Not set by default, does not fall back to ``SERVER_NAME``.
functions.code:
def get_cookie_domain(self, app: Flask) -> str | None:
        """The value of the ``Domain`` parameter on the session cookie. If not set,
        browsers will only send the cookie to the exact domain it was set from.
        Otherwise, they will send it to any subdomain of the given value as well.

        Uses the :data:`SESSION_COOKIE_DOMAIN` config.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.3
            Not set by default, does not fall back to ``SERVER_NAME``.
        """
        return app.config["SESSION_COOKIE_DOMAIN"]

functions.name: get_cookie_path
functions.docstring:
Returns the path for which the cookie should be valid.  The
default implementation uses the value from the ``SESSION_COOKIE_PATH``
config var if it's set, and falls back to ``APPLICATION_ROOT`` or
uses ``/`` if it's ``None``.
functions.code:
def get_cookie_path(self, app: Flask) -> str:
        """Returns the path for which the cookie should be valid.  The
        default implementation uses the value from the ``SESSION_COOKIE_PATH``
        config var if it's set, and falls back to ``APPLICATION_ROOT`` or
        uses ``/`` if it's ``None``.
        """
        return app.config["SESSION_COOKIE_PATH"] or app.config["APPLICATION_ROOT"]

currently just returns the value of the ``SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY``

        config var.

        return app.config["SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY"]

functions.name: get_expiration_time
functions.docstring:
A helper method that returns an expiration date for the session
or ``None`` if the session is linked to the browser session.  The
default implementation returns now + the permanent session
lifetime configured on the application.
functions.code:
def get_expiration_time(self, app: Flask, session: SessionMixin) -> datetime | None:
        """A helper method that returns an expiration date for the session
        or ``None`` if the session is linked to the browser session.  The
        default implementation returns now + the permanent session
        lifetime configured on the application.
        """
        if session.permanent:
            return datetime.now(timezone.utc) + app.permanent_session_lifetime
        return None

functions.name: should_set_cookie
functions.docstring:
Used by session backends to determine if a ``Set-Cookie`` header
should be set for this session cookie for this response. If the session
has been modified, the cookie is set. If the session is permanent and
the ``SESSION_REFRESH_EACH_REQUEST`` config is true, the cookie is
always set.

This check is usually skipped if the session was deleted.

.. versionadded:: 0.11
functions.code:
def should_set_cookie(self, app: Flask, session: SessionMixin) -> bool:
        """Used by session backends to determine if a ``Set-Cookie`` header
        should be set for this session cookie for this response. If the session
        has been modified, the cookie is set. If the session is permanent and
        the ``SESSION_REFRESH_EACH_REQUEST`` config is true, the cookie is
        always set.

        This check is usually skipped if the session was deleted.

        .. versionadded:: 0.11
        """

        return session.modified or (
            session.permanent and app.config["SESSION_REFRESH_EACH_REQUEST"]
        )

functions.name: open_session
functions.docstring:
This is called at the beginning of each request, after
pushing the request context, before matching the URL.

This must return an object which implements a dictionary-like
interface as well as the :class:`SessionMixin` interface.

This will return ``None`` to indicate that loading failed in
some way that is not immediately an error. The request
context will fall back to using :meth:`make_null_session`
in this case.
functions.code:
def open_session(self, app: Flask, request: Request) -> SessionMixin | None:
        """This is called at the beginning of each request, after
        pushing the request context, before matching the URL.

        This must return an object which implements a dictionary-like
        interface as well as the :class:`SessionMixin` interface.

        This will return ``None`` to indicate that loading failed in
        some way that is not immediately an error. The request
        context will fall back to using :meth:`make_null_session`
        in this case.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

functions.name: save_session
functions.docstring:
This is called at the end of each request, after generating
a response, before removing the request context. It is skipped
if :meth:`is_null_session` returns ``True``.
functions.code:
def save_session(
        self, app: Flask, session: SessionMixin, response: Response
    ) -> None:
        """This is called at the end of each request, after generating
        a response, before removing the request context. It is skipped
        if :meth:`is_null_session` returns ``True``.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

functions.name: get_signing_serializer
open_session
save_session
functions.code:
def save_session(
        self, app: Flask, session: SessionMixin, response: Response
    ) -> None:
        name = self.get_cookie_name(app)
        domain = self.get_cookie_domain(app)
        path = self.get_cookie_path(app)
        secure = self.get_cookie_secure(app)
        partitioned = self.get_cookie_partitioned(app)
        samesite = self.get_cookie_samesite(app)
        httponly = self.get_cookie_httponly(app)

        # Add a "Vary: Cookie" header if the session was accessed at all.
        if session.accessed:
            response.vary.add("Cookie")

        # If the session is modified to be empty, remove the cookie.
        # If the session is empty, return without setting the cookie.
        if not session:
            if session.modified:
                response.delete_cookie(
                    name,
                    domain=domain,
                    path=path,
                    secure=secure,
                    partitioned=partitioned,
                    samesite=samesite,
                    httponly=httponly,
                )
                response.vary.add("Cookie")

            return

        if not self.should_set_cookie(app, session):
            return

        expires = self.get_expiration_time(app, session)
        val = self.get_signing_serializer(app).dumps(dict(session))  # type: ignore[union-attr]
        response.set_cookie(
            name,
            val,
            expires=expires,
            httponly=httponly,
            domain=domain,
            path=path,
            secure=secure,
            partitioned=partitioned,
            samesite=samesite,
        )
        response.vary.add("Cookie")

classes.name: SecureCookieSession
classes.docstring:
Base class for sessions based on signed cookies.

This session backend will set the :attr:`modified` and
:attr:`accessed` attributes. It cannot reliably track whether a
session is new (vs. empty), so :attr:`new` remains hard coded to
``False``.

classes.name: NullSession
classes.docstring:
Class used to generate nicer error messages if sessions are not
available.  Will still allow read-only access to the empty session
but fail on setting.

classes.name: SessionInterface
classes.docstring:
The basic interface you have to implement in order to replace the
default session interface which uses werkzeug's securecookie
implementation.  The only methods you have to implement are
:meth:`open_session` and :meth:`save_session`, the others have
useful defaults which you don't need to change.

The session object returned by the :meth:`open_session` method has to
provide a dictionary like interface plus the properties and methods
from the :class:`SessionMixin`.  We recommend just subclassing a dict
and adding that mixin::

    class Session(dict, SessionMixin):
        pass

If :meth:`open_session` returns ``None`` Flask will call into
:meth:`make_null_session` to create a session that acts as replacement
if the session support cannot work because some requirement is not
fulfilled.  The default :class:`NullSession` class that is created
will complain that the secret key was not set.

To replace the session interface on an application all you have to do
is to assign :attr:`flask.Flask.session_interface`::

    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.session_interface = MySessionInterface()

Multiple requests with the same session may be sent and handled
concurrently. When implementing a new session interface, consider
whether reads or writes to the backing store must be synchronized.
There is no guarantee on the order in which the session for each
request is opened or saved, it will occur in the order that requests
begin and end processing.

.. versionadded:: 0.8

classes.name: SecureCookieSessionInterface

The default session interface that stores sessions in signed cookies
through the :mod:`itsdangerous` module.

functions.name: _render
functions.code:
def _render(ctx: AppContext, template: Template, context: dict[str, t.Any]) -> str:
    app = ctx.app
    app.update_template_context(ctx, context)
    before_render_template.send(
        app, _async_wrapper=app.ensure_sync, template=template, context=context
    )
    rv = template.render(context)
    template_rendered.send(
        app, _async_wrapper=app.ensure_sync, template=template, context=context
    )
    return rv

functions.docstring:
Render a template by name with the given context.

:param template_name_or_list: The name of the template to render. If
    a list is given, the first name to exist will be rendered.
:param context: The variables to make available in the template.
functions.code:
def render_template(
    template_name_or_list: str | Template | list[str | Template],
    **context: t.Any,
) -> str:
    """Render a template by name with the given context.

    :param template_name_or_list: The name of the template to render. If
        a list is given, the first name to exist will be rendered.
    :param context: The variables to make available in the template.
    """
    ctx = app_ctx._get_current_object()
    template = ctx.app.jinja_env.get_or_select_template(template_name_or_list)
    return _render(ctx, template, context)

    ctx = app_ctx._get_current_object()

    return _render(ctx, template, context)

functions.name: _get_werkzeug_version
__init__
functions.code:
def __init__(
        self,
        app: Flask,
        path: str = "/",
        base_url: str | None = None,
        subdomain: str | None = None,
        url_scheme: str | None = None,
        *args: t.Any,
        **kwargs: t.Any,
    ) -> None:
        assert not (base_url or subdomain or url_scheme) or (
            base_url is not None
        ) != bool(subdomain or url_scheme), (
            'Cannot pass "subdomain" or "url_scheme" with "base_url".'
        )

        if base_url is None:
            http_host = app.config.get("SERVER_NAME") or "localhost"
            app_root = app.config["APPLICATION_ROOT"]

            if subdomain:
                http_host = f"{subdomain}.{http_host}"

            if url_scheme is None:
                url_scheme = app.config["PREFERRED_URL_SCHEME"]

            url = urlsplit(path)
            base_url = (
                f"{url.scheme or url_scheme}://{url.netloc or http_host}"
                f"/{app_root.lstrip('/')}"
            )
            path = url.path

            if url.query:
                path = f"{path}?{url.query}"

        self.app = app
        super().__init__(path, base_url, *args, **kwargs)

functions.name: __init__
session_transaction
functions.docstring:
When used in combination with a ``with`` statement this opens a
session transaction.  This can be used to modify the session that
the test client uses.  Once the ``with`` block is left the session is
stored back.

::

    with client.session_transaction() as session:
        session['value'] = 42

Internally this is implemented by going through a temporary test
request context and since session handling could depend on
request variables this function accepts the same arguments as
:meth:`~flask.Flask.test_request_context` which are directly
passed through.
functions.code:
def session_transaction(
        self, *args: t.Any, **kwargs: t.Any
    ) -> t.Iterator[SessionMixin]:
        """When used in combination with a ``with`` statement this opens a
        session transaction.  This can be used to modify the session that
        the test client uses.  Once the ``with`` block is left the session is
        stored back.

        ::

            with client.session_transaction() as session:
                session['value'] = 42

        Internally this is implemented by going through a temporary test
        request context and since session handling could depend on
        request variables this function accepts the same arguments as
        :meth:`~flask.Flask.test_request_context` which are directly
        passed through.
        """
        if self._cookies is None:
            raise TypeError(
                "Cookies are disabled. Create a client with 'use_cookies=True'."
            )

        app = self.application
        ctx = app.test_request_context(*args, **kwargs)
        self._add_cookies_to_wsgi(ctx.request.environ)

        with ctx:
            sess = app.session_interface.open_session(app, ctx.request)

        if sess is None:
            raise RuntimeError("Session backend did not open a session.")

        yield sess
        resp = app.response_class()

        if app.session_interface.is_null_session(sess):
            return

        with ctx:
            app.session_interface.save_session(app, sess, resp)

        self._update_cookies_from_response(
            ctx.request.host.partition(":")[0],
            ctx.request.path,
            resp.headers.getlist("Set-Cookie"),
        )

functions.name: _copy_environ
_request_from_builder_args
open
functions.code:
def open(
        self,
        *args: t.Any,
        buffered: bool = False,
        follow_redirects: bool = False,
        **kwargs: t.Any,
    ) -> TestResponse:
        if args and isinstance(
            args[0], (werkzeug.test.EnvironBuilder, dict, BaseRequest)
        ):
            if isinstance(args[0], werkzeug.test.EnvironBuilder):
                builder = copy(args[0])
                builder.environ_base = self._copy_environ(builder.environ_base or {})  # type: ignore[arg-type]
                request = builder.get_request()
            elif isinstance(args[0], dict):
                request = EnvironBuilder.from_environ(
                    args[0], app=self.application, environ_base=self._copy_environ({})
                ).get_request()
            else:
                # isinstance(args[0], BaseRequest)
                request = copy(args[0])
                request.environ = self._copy_environ(request.environ)
        else:
            # request is None
            request = self._request_from_builder_args(args, kwargs)

        # Pop any previously preserved contexts. This prevents contexts
        # from being preserved across redirects or multiple requests
        # within a single block.
        self._context_stack.close()

        response = super().open(
            request,
            buffered=buffered,
            follow_redirects=follow_redirects,
        )
        response.json_module = self.application.json  # type: ignore[assignment]

        # Re-push contexts that were preserved during the request.
        for cm in self._new_contexts:
            self._context_stack.enter_context(cm)

        self._new_contexts.clear()
        return response

functions.name: __init__
invoke
functions.docstring:
Invokes a CLI command in an isolated environment. See
:meth:`CliRunner.invoke <click.testing.CliRunner.invoke>` for
full method documentation. See :ref:`testing-cli` for examples.

If the ``obj`` argument is not given, passes an instance of
:class:`~flask.cli.ScriptInfo` that knows how to load the Flask
app being tested.

:param cli: Command object to invoke. Default is the app's
    :attr:`~flask.app.Flask.cli` group.
:param args: List of strings to invoke the command with.

:return: a :class:`~click.testing.Result` object.
functions.code:
def invoke(  # type: ignore
        self, cli: t.Any = None, args: t.Any = None, **kwargs: t.Any
    ) -> Result:
        """Invokes a CLI command in an isolated environment. See
        :meth:`CliRunner.invoke <click.testing.CliRunner.invoke>` for
        full method documentation. See :ref:`testing-cli` for examples.

        If the ``obj`` argument is not given, passes an instance of
        :class:`~flask.cli.ScriptInfo` that knows how to load the Flask
        app being tested.

        :param cli: Command object to invoke. Default is the app's
            :attr:`~flask.app.Flask.cli` group.
        :param args: List of strings to invoke the command with.

        :return: a :class:`~click.testing.Result` object.
        """
        if cli is None:
            cli = self.app.cli

        if "obj" not in kwargs:
            kwargs["obj"] = ScriptInfo(create_app=lambda: self.app)

        return super().invoke(cli, args, **kwargs)

functions.name: as_view
functions.docstring:
Convert the class into a view function that can be registered
for a route.

By default, the generated view will create a new instance of the
view class for every request and call its
:meth:`dispatch_request` method. If the view class sets
:attr:`init_every_request` to ``False``, the same instance will
be used for every request.

Except for ``name``, all other arguments passed to this method
are forwarded to the view class ``__init__`` method.

.. versionchanged:: 2.2
    Added the ``init_every_request`` class attribute.
functions.code:
def as_view(
        cls, name: str, *class_args: t.Any, **class_kwargs: t.Any
    ) -> ft.RouteCallable:
        """Convert the class into a view function that can be registered
        for a route.

        By default, the generated view will create a new instance of the
        view class for every request and call its
        :meth:`dispatch_request` method. If the view class sets
        :attr:`init_every_request` to ``False``, the same instance will
        be used for every request.

        Except for ``name``, all other arguments passed to this method
        are forwarded to the view class ``__init__`` method.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.2
            Added the ``init_every_request`` class attribute.
        """
        if cls.init_every_request:

            def view(**kwargs: t.Any) -> ft.ResponseReturnValue:
                self = view.view_class(  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
                    *class_args, **class_kwargs
                )
                return current_app.ensure_sync(self.dispatch_request)(**kwargs)  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        else:
            self = cls(*class_args, **class_kwargs)  # pyright: ignore

            def view(**kwargs: t.Any) -> ft.ResponseReturnValue:
                return current_app.ensure_sync(self.dispatch_request)(**kwargs)  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        if cls.decorators:
            view.__name__ = name
            view.__module__ = cls.__module__
            for decorator in cls.decorators:
                view = decorator(view)

        # We attach the view class to the view function for two reasons:
        # first of all it allows us to easily figure out what class-based
        # view this thing came from, secondly it's also used for instantiating
        # the view class so you can actually replace it with something else
        # for testing purposes and debugging.
        view.view_class = cls  # type: ignore
        view.__name__ = name
        view.__doc__ = cls.__doc__
        view.__module__ = cls.__module__
        view.methods = cls.methods  # type: ignore
        view.provide_automatic_options = cls.provide_automatic_options  # type: ignore
        return view

classes.name: View
classes.docstring:
Subclass this class and override :meth:`dispatch_request` to
create a generic class-based view. Call :meth:`as_view` to create a
view function that creates an instance of the class with the given
arguments and calls its ``dispatch_request`` method with any URL
variables.

See :doc:`views` for a detailed guide.

.. code-block:: python

    class Hello(View):
        init_every_request = False

        def dispatch_request(self, name):
            return f"Hello, {name}!"

    app.add_url_rule(
        "/hello/<name>", view_func=Hello.as_view("hello")
    )

Set :attr:`methods` on the class to change what methods the view
accepts.

Set :attr:`decorators` on the class to apply a list of decorators to
the generated view function. Decorators applied to the class itself
will not be applied to the generated view function!

Set :attr:`init_every_request` to ``False`` for efficiency, unless
you need to store request-global data on ``self``.

functions.name: max_content_length
functions.docstring:
The maximum number of bytes that will be read during this request. If
this limit is exceeded, a 413 :exc:`~werkzeug.exceptions.RequestEntityTooLarge`
error is raised. If it is set to ``None``, no limit is enforced at the
Flask application level. However, if it is ``None`` and the request has
no ``Content-Length`` header and the WSGI server does not indicate that
it terminates the stream, then no data is read to avoid an infinite
stream.

Each request defaults to the :data:`MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH` config, which
defaults to ``None``. It can be set on a specific ``request`` to apply
the limit to that specific view. This should be set appropriately based
on an application's or view's specific needs.

.. versionchanged:: 3.1
    This can be set per-request.

.. versionchanged:: 0.6
    This is configurable through Flask config.
functions.code:
def max_content_length(self) -> int | None:
        """The maximum number of bytes that will be read during this request. If
        this limit is exceeded, a 413 :exc:`~werkzeug.exceptions.RequestEntityTooLarge`
        error is raised. If it is set to ``None``, no limit is enforced at the
        Flask application level. However, if it is ``None`` and the request has
        no ``Content-Length`` header and the WSGI server does not indicate that
        it terminates the stream, then no data is read to avoid an infinite
        stream.

        Each request defaults to the :data:`MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH` config, which
        defaults to ``None``. It can be set on a specific ``request`` to apply
        the limit to that specific view. This should be set appropriately based
        on an application's or view's specific needs.

        .. versionchanged:: 3.1
            This can be set per-request.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.6
            This is configurable through Flask config.
        """
        if self._max_content_length is not None:
            return self._max_content_length

        if not current_app:
            return super().max_content_length

        return current_app.config["MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH"]

This in combination with :attr:`view_args` can be used to

functions.code:
def endpoint(self) -> str | None:
        """The endpoint that matched the request URL.

        This will be ``None`` if matching failed or has not been
        performed yet.

        This in combination with :attr:`view_args` can be used to
        reconstruct the same URL or a modified URL.
        """
        if self.url_rule is not None:
            return self.url_rule.endpoint  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        return None

functions.docstring:
The registered name of the current blueprint.

This will be ``None`` if the endpoint is not part of a
blueprint, or if URL matching failed or has not been performed
yet.

This does not necessarily match the name the blueprint was
created with. It may have been nested, or registered with a
different name.
functions.code:
def blueprint(self) -> str | None:
        """The registered name of the current blueprint.

        This will be ``None`` if the endpoint is not part of a
        blueprint, or if URL matching failed or has not been performed
        yet.

        This does not necessarily match the name the blueprint was
        created with. It may have been nested, or registered with a
        different name.
        """
        endpoint = self.endpoint

        if endpoint is not None and "." in endpoint:
            return endpoint.rpartition(".")[0]

        return None

functions.name: blueprints
functions.docstring:
The registered names of the current blueprint upwards through
parent blueprints.

This will be an empty list if there is no current blueprint, or
if URL matching failed.

.. versionadded:: 2.0.1
functions.code:
def blueprints(self) -> list[str]:
        """The registered names of the current blueprint upwards through
        parent blueprints.

        This will be an empty list if there is no current blueprint, or
        if URL matching failed.

        .. versionadded:: 2.0.1
        """
        name = self.blueprint

        if name is None:
            return []

        return _split_blueprint_path(name)

functions.name: _load_form_data
functions.code:
def _load_form_data(self) -> None:
        super()._load_form_data()

        # In debug mode we're replacing the files multidict with an ad-hoc
        # subclass that raises a different error for key errors.
        if (
            current_app
            and current_app.debug
            and self.mimetype != "multipart/form-data"
            and not self.files
        ):
            from .debughelpers import attach_enctype_error_multidict

            attach_enctype_error_multidict(self)

functions.name: on_json_loading_failed
max_cookie_size
functions.docstring:
Read-only view of the :data:`MAX_COOKIE_SIZE` config key.

See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Response.max_cookie_size` in
Werkzeug's docs.
functions.code:
def max_cookie_size(self) -> int:  # type: ignore
        """Read-only view of the :data:`MAX_COOKIE_SIZE` config key.

        See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Response.max_cookie_size` in
        Werkzeug's docs.
        """
        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_COOKIE_SIZE"]  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        # return Werkzeug's default when not in an app context
        return super().max_cookie_size

classes.name: Request
classes.docstring:
The request object used by default in Flask.  Remembers the
matched endpoint and view arguments.

It is what ends up as :class:`~flask.request`.  If you want to replace
the request object used you can subclass this and set
:attr:`~flask.Flask.request_class` to your subclass.

The request object is a :class:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Request` subclass and
provides all of the attributes Werkzeug defines plus a few Flask
specific ones.

classes.name: Response
classes.docstring:
The response object that is used by default in Flask.  Works like the
response object from Werkzeug but is set to have an HTML mimetype by
default.  Quite often you don't have to create this object yourself because
:meth:`~flask.Flask.make_response` will take care of that for you.

If you want to replace the response object used you can subclass this and
set :attr:`~flask.Flask.response_class` to your subclass.

.. versionchanged:: 1.0
    JSON support is added to the response, like the request. This is useful
    when testing to get the test client response data as JSON.

.. versionchanged:: 1.0

    Added :attr:`max_cookie_size`.

github_link.human: This function creates a clickable link to the Flask GitHub repository that automatically points to the correct branch based on the current software version. If you're using a development version, it links to the "main" branch; otherwise, it links to the specific release version. It's a documentation helper that allows writers to reference GitHub files without manually updating URLs when versions change.
github_link.technical: A Sphinx role function that parses inline reStructuredText markup, extracts target path and optional display text (delimited by `<>`), constructs a GitHub URL using the app's release version (checking if it's a dev release via `packaging.version.parse`), and returns a docutils reference node. It sets CSS classes via `set_classes()` and returns the node and empty message list for Sphinx processing.

create_app.human: This function sets up a Flask web application with background job processing capabilities. It configures the app with a Redis-based task queue system, creates a home page route, and registers additional view modules. The function essentially builds and returns a fully initialized web application ready to handle requests and background tasks.
create_app.technical: Creates a Flask app instance and configures it with Celery settings (Redis broker/backend) via `app.config.from_mapping()`, then loads environment-based overrides with `app.config.from_prefixed_env()`. Initializes Celery with `celery_init_app(app)`, registers a root route decorator that renders `index.html`, imports and registers a blueprint from the `views` module via `app.register_blueprint()`, and returns the configured Flask application object.

celery_init_app.human: This function sets up Celery (a task scheduling system) to work with a Flask web application. It ensures that background tasks can access the Flask application's context and configuration, allowing them to function properly within the app's environment. It registers the configured Celery instance so the Flask app can use it for running asynchronous jobs.
celery_init_app.technical: Creates a custom `FlaskTask` class that wraps task execution within Flask's application context, instantiates a Celery app using the Flask app's name and custom task class, loads Celery configuration from the Flask app's "CELERY" config dictionary, sets it as the default Celery instance globally, stores the Celery instance in Flask extensions, and returns the initialized Celery object for use throughout the application.

__call__.human: This function makes an object callable (like a function) by wrapping its execution within a Flask application context. When someone calls this object, it ensures the Flask app environment is properly set up before running the actual operation. This is useful for Flask tasks that need the app's context to access databases, configurations, or other app-specific resources.
__call__.technical: The `__call__` method enables the object to be invoked as a callable. It establishes a Flask application context using `app.app_context()`, then delegates execution to `self.run(*args, **kwargs)` within that context. All arguments and keyword arguments are passed through transparently, and the return value from `self.run()` is returned to the caller. This pattern ensures context-dependent Flask operations execute in the proper application environment.

process.human: This function simulates a long-running task that reports its progress over time. It loops through iterations, pausing briefly between each step, and continuously updates a progress tracker so the user can monitor how much work is complete. Once all iterations finish, it returns a completion confirmation.
process.technical: A Celery shared_task that iterates from 0 to `total`, calling `self.update_state()` on each iteration to report progress metadata (current count and total). Each iteration sleeps for 1 second, creating an observable delay. Finally returns a dictionary with `current` and `total` keys confirming task completion. Designed for async task monitoring in Celery-based applications.

result.human: This function checks the status of an asynchronous task by its ID. It returns whether the task is complete, whether it succeeded (if complete), and retrieves the task's result or return value. It's useful for polling long-running operations to see if they've finished and what outcome they produced.
result.technical: Creates an AsyncResult object from the provided task ID and checks if it's ready using `result.ready()`. Returns a dictionary containing: the ready status, the success status (only if ready), and either the final result via `result.get()` or the pending result attribute. This is a Flask blueprint GET endpoint that provides task status introspection for Celery-like async task systems.

add.human: This function accepts two numbers from a web form submission and queues them for addition in the background. Instead of calculating the result immediately, it returns a task ID that the user can use to check on the progress and retrieve the result later. This allows the web server to respond quickly without waiting for the calculation to complete.
add.technical: Extracts two integer parameters ("a" and "b") from HTTP POST form data, dispatches an asynchronous task to `tasks.add.delay()` (a Celery task queue), and returns a dictionary containing the task's unique ID. The `@bp.post` decorator registers this as a POST endpoint on a Flask blueprint. The function enables asynchronous processing by returning immediately with a result identifier rather than blocking on computation.

block.human: This function triggers a blocking task to run asynchronously in the background and immediately returns a tracking ID. It allows the caller to monitor or retrieve results of the long-running operation later without waiting for it to complete. This is useful for time-consuming operations that shouldn't freeze the application.
block.technical: The function invokes `tasks.block.delay()` (a Celery async task) which queues the task for background execution and returns a task result object. It extracts the task's unique ID from the result object and returns it as a dictionary with key `"result_id"`. The `@bp.post` decorator exposes this as an HTTP POST endpoint, enabling remote clients to trigger async jobs and receive task IDs for polling.

process.human: This function receives a numerical value from a user request and submits it to a background processing task. Instead of waiting for the processing to complete, it immediately returns a unique identifier that the user can use to track the status of their request later. This enables fast, non-blocking request handling for long-running operations.
process.technical: Extracts the "total" parameter from HTTP form data (converted to integer), passes it to a Celery task via `tasks.process.delay()` for asynchronous execution, and returns a dictionary containing the task's unique ID. The `@bp.post` decorator registers this as a POST endpoint. The function returns immediately without waiting for task completion, enabling non-blocking async job submission.

add.human: This function is a web service endpoint that takes two numbers as input and returns their sum. It retrieves two values from a form submission, adds them together, and sends back the result in a structured response format that web applications can easily consume.
add.technical: A Flask route handler that extracts two float parameters ("a" and "b") from form data using `request.form.get()` with default value 0, performs arithmetic addition, and returns the result as JSON via `jsonify()`. No side effects; simple pure computation wrapped in HTTP/JSON interface for client-server communication.

create_app.human: This function sets up a Flask web application by configuring it with default settings, loading environment-specific configurations, and registering all the essential components like the database and feature modules. It's the initialization routine that prepares the entire application before it can run and handle requests.
create_app.technical: Creates a Flask app instance with instance-relative config, sets default config values (SECRET_KEY, DATABASE path), loads config from file or test settings via `app.config.from_pyfile()` or `app.config.update()`, initializes the database with `db.init_app()`, registers auth and blog blueprints via `app.register_blueprint()`, adds a "/hello" route, maps the root URL "/" to the blog index endpoint, and returns the configured app object.

login_required.human: This function acts as a security guard for web pages. It checks if a user is logged in before allowing them to view a page. If someone who isn't logged in tries to access a protected page, they get redirected to the login page instead. It's a simple way to keep unauthorized users out of private areas of a website.
login_required.technical: A decorator function that wraps view functions to enforce authentication. It checks if `g.user` is `None` (indicating no logged-in user) and redirects to the login route via `url_for("auth.login")` if so. Uses `functools.wraps` to preserve the original view's metadata, accepts `**kwargs` to pass through URL parameters, and returns either a redirect response or the result of calling the original view function.

load_logged_in_user.human: This function automatically checks if someone is logged in whenever a web request arrives. If they are logged in, it retrieves their user information from the database and makes it available to the rest of the application. If they're not logged in, it clears any user information. This happens behind the scenes for every request so the app always knows who the current user is.
load_logged_in_user.technical: Executes as a before_app_request hook that retrieves the "user_id" from the Flask session. If user_id exists, it queries the database with `get_db().execute("SELECT * FROM user WHERE id = ?", (user_id,)).fetchone()` and stores the user record in Flask's `g.user` context variable; otherwise sets `g.user` to None. No return value; operates entirely through side effects on the global `g` object.

register.human: This function allows new users to create an account on the platform. It collects a username and password from a registration form, validates that both are provided and that the username isn't already in use, then securely stores the new user in the database. If registration succeeds, it redirects to the login page; if it fails, it displays an error message.
register.technical: Handles POST requests by extracting username and password from form data, validating non-empty inputs, then executing an INSERT query with a hashed password using `generate_password_hash()`. Catches `db.IntegrityError` exceptions (duplicate username constraint violation) to display appropriate errors via `flash()`. On success, commits the transaction and redirects to the login route; on failure or GET requests, renders the registration template.

login.human: This function allows users to log in to their account by verifying their username and password. It checks if the credentials are correct against the database, and if they are, it creates a session for the user so they stay logged in. If the credentials are wrong, it displays an error message. The function also displays the login form for users to enter their credentials.
login.technical: Handles POST requests by extracting username and password from form data, querying the database for the user record, and validating credentials using `check_password_hash()`. On successful authentication, clears any existing session, stores the user ID in `session["user_id"]`, and redirects to the index page. On validation failure, flashes an error message. Always renders the login template for GET requests or when authentication fails.

logout.human: This function logs a user out of the application by erasing all their stored session data (like their user ID). After clearing everything, it redirects them back to the home page, completing the logout process and ensuring they're no longer authenticated in the system.
logout.technical: The function calls `session.clear()` to remove all session data stored server-side, then returns a redirect response using `redirect(url_for("index"))` to navigate the user to the index page. It's registered as a Flask route via the `bp.route` decorator, making it an HTTP endpoint. The function has no parameters and returns None (the redirect object is the actual HTTP response).

wrapped_view.human: This function acts as a security guard for protected pages. It checks whether a user is logged in before allowing them to access a page. If no one is logged in, it redirects them to the login page; otherwise, it lets them proceed to the page they requested.
wrapped_view.technical: A decorator wrapper that checks if `g.user` is `None` (indicating no authenticated user). If the user is not authenticated, it redirects to the "auth.login" route using `url_for()` and `redirect()`. Otherwise, it calls the wrapped `view()` function with all passed keyword arguments and returns its result. The decorator uses `functools.wraps` to preserve the original function's metadata.

index.human: This function retrieves all blog posts from the database and displays them on the homepage. It fetches each post along with its author's information, ordering them so the newest posts appear first. The posts are then passed to a template that renders them as a web page for users to view.
index.technical: Executes a SQL JOIN query combining `post` and `user` tables to fetch post metadata (id, title, body, created timestamp, author_id) with the associated username, sorted by creation date in descending order. Calls `get_db()` to obtain database connection, chains `.fetchall()` to retrieve all result rows, and passes the post list to Flask's `render_template()` to render the "blog/index.html" template with the posts data. Returns the rendered HTML response.

get_post.human: This function retrieves a blog post from the database by its ID and verifies the current user has permission to access it. It checks that the post exists and optionally confirms the logged-in user is the post's author. If either check fails, it denies access; otherwise, it returns the post's details including the author information.
get_post.technical: Queries the database using a JOIN between `post` and `user` tables to fetch post content and author details by post ID. Calls `abort(404)` if the post doesn't exist, and `abort(403)` if `check_author` is True and the current user's ID doesn't match the post's author ID. Returns the post record as a dictionary-like object from `fetchone()`.

create.human: This function allows logged-in users to create and publish a new blog post. It displays a form for users to enter a title and body text, validates that a title is provided, and saves the post to the database with the user's information. After successful creation, the user is redirected back to the blog's main page.
create.technical: Handles both GET and POST requests: GET renders the creation form template, POST validates form input (requiring a non-empty title), executes an INSERT query to store the post in the database with the current user's ID, commits the transaction, and redirects to the blog index. Uses Flask decorators for routing and login protection, with error handling that flashes validation messages to the user.

update.human: This function allows users to edit blog posts they've written. When accessed via POST request, it takes the updated title and body from a form, validates that the title isn't empty, saves the changes to the database, and redirects back to the main blog page. If accessed via GET request, it displays the edit form pre-populated with the post's current content.
update.technical: Retrieves the post by ID using `get_post()`, then handles two request methods: POST validates form inputs (title/body from `request.form`), executes a SQL UPDATE query via `db.execute()`, commits changes with `db.commit()`, and redirects to blog index; GET renders the update template with the post data. Uses Flask's `login_required` decorator for authentication and `flash()` for error messaging. Returns a redirect response (POST) or rendered HTML template (GET).

delete.human: This function allows a logged-in user to permanently remove a blog post they created. It first verifies the post exists and that the current user is the author (via `get_post()`), then deletes the post from the database and redirects the user back to the main blog page. It's a cleanup operation that prevents unauthorized deletion.
delete.technical: Calls `get_post(id)` for authorization/validation checks, retrieves the database connection via `get_db()`, executes a DELETE SQL query to remove the post by id, commits the transaction to persist changes, and returns a redirect response to "blog.index" URL. Protected by `@login_required` decorator to ensure only authenticated users can access it.

get_db.human: This function provides a single database connection for each user request. It checks if a connection already exists for the current request, and if not, it creates a new one using the application's database configuration. This ensures efficient reuse of database connections rather than creating new ones repeatedly. The function returns the database connection object ready for queries.
get_db.technical: Implements lazy-loaded database connection caching using Flask's `g` object (request-scoped storage). On first call, establishes SQLite connection via `sqlite3.connect()` with `PARSE_DECLTYPES` flag enabled for type detection, configures `row_factory` to return `sqlite3.Row` objects for dict-like row access, and stores in `g.db`. Subsequent calls within the same request return the cached connection without reconnecting. Returns the database connection object.

close_db.human: This function safely closes the database connection when a web request finishes. It checks if the current request actually opened a database connection, and if so, closes it to free up resources. This prevents database connections from staying open unnecessarily or causing memory leaks.
close_db.technical: Uses Flask's `g` object to retrieve a "db" key (returns `None` if not present via `pop()`). If a database connection exists, calls its `close()` method to cleanly terminate it. Designed as a teardown handler that executes after each request completes, accepting an optional exception parameter `e` for error handling integration.

init_db.human: This function resets the application's database to a clean state by removing all existing data and setting up a fresh database structure. It reads database schema instructions from a file and applies them to create the necessary tables. This is typically used when initializing a new application or testing scenarios where you need a predictable starting database state.
init_db.technical: Retrieves the active database connection via `get_db()`, then loads the SQL schema file using `current_app.open_resource("schema.sql")`. Reads the file content, decodes it from UTF-8 bytes to string, and executes all SQL commands in the schema using `db.executescript()`. This approach handles multiple SQL statements in a single batch operation, clearing old data and creating fresh tables as defined in the schema file.

init_db_command.human: This function resets the application's database to a clean state by removing all existing data and creating fresh tables. It's a setup/initialization command that prepares the database for use, typically run once during application installation or when you need to start over with a blank database.
init_db_command.technical: A Click CLI command decorator that executes two operations: calls `init_db()` to perform the actual database reset (dropping and recreating tables), then uses `click.echo()` to output a confirmation message to the user. Returns None and has side effects of modifying the database state and printing to stdout.

init_app.human: This function sets up the database system for a Flask web application. It registers two important tasks: one that closes the database connection when the application shuts down, and another that adds a command-line tool for initializing the database. This ensures the database is properly managed throughout the app's lifecycle.
init_app.technical: The function registers two handlers with the Flask app object: `app.teardown_appcontext(close_db)` registers a cleanup function to execute after each request context ends, ensuring database connections are closed; `app.cli.add_command(init_db_command)` adds a CLI command for database initialization. The function has no return value but modifies the app's configuration through side effects.

_make_timedelta.human: This function normalizes time duration values into a standard format. It accepts either a time duration object, a number of seconds, or nothing (None), and converts them all into a consistent timedelta object. This is useful when a function needs to work with time durations regardless of how the user provides them.
_make_timedelta.technical: The function performs type checking and conversion on the input value. If the value is None or already a timedelta object, it returns unchanged. Otherwise, it treats the input as seconds and constructs a new timedelta object via `timedelta(seconds=value)`. Returns either a timedelta instance or None, with no side effects beyond the return value.

remove_ctx.human: This function wraps another function to filter out Flask application context objects from the arguments before passing them along. It solves the problem of handling cases where an AppContext is unexpectedly included in the arguments—by removing it transparently, the wrapped function receives only the arguments it actually needs.
remove_ctx.technical: The decorator creates a wrapper that intercepts calls to function `f` with a Flask instance as `self`. It checks if the first positional argument is an `AppContext` instance and removes it from `args` if present. The wrapper then calls the original function with the cleaned arguments and returns its result. Finally, `update_wrapper` copies metadata from the original function to the wrapper, maintaining function identity for introspection.

add_ctx.human: This function automatically injects the current application context into Flask method calls. It ensures that whenever a decorated function is called, it receives the active application context as its first argument (after `self`), even if the caller didn't explicitly provide one. This solves the problem of requiring developers to manually pass the context every time.
add_ctx.technical: The wrapper intercepts function calls and inspects the `args` tuple: if empty or if the first argument isn't an `AppContext` instance, it prepends the current app context obtained via `app_ctx._get_current_object()`. It then calls the original function `f` with the modified arguments and returns the result. The `update_wrapper` call copies metadata from the original function to preserve its identity and documentation.

wrapper.human: This function ensures that an application context is always available when executing wrapped code. It automatically injects the current Flask application context as the first argument if one isn't already provided, allowing the wrapped function to reliably access Flask's context information. This solves the problem of functions needing context but being called from different contexts.
wrapper.technical: The wrapper checks if arguments are empty or if the first argument isn't an AppContext instance. If either condition is true, it prepends the current app context retrieved via `app_ctx._get_current_object()` to the args tuple. It then calls the wrapped function `f` with the modified arguments and all kwargs, returning its result. This ensures consistent context availability regardless of how the function is invoked.

__init_subclass__.human: This function automatically detects when developers override certain Flask methods in subclasses using outdated function signatures. When it finds an old-style method, it issues a deprecation warning to alert the developer that the signature has changed, then wraps the method to work with the new signature anyway—ensuring backward compatibility while encouraging developers to update their code.
__init_subclass__.technical: Iterates through 12 core Flask methods, comparing each subclass override against the base Flask implementation. For each overridden method, inspects the second parameter using `inspect.signature()` to verify it's named `ctx` or annotated with `AppContext` (checking string, class, or direct annotations). If the check fails, issues a `DeprecationWarning` and wraps the method with `remove_ctx()` on the subclass and `add_ctx()` on the base class to bridge the signature mismatch.

__init__.human: This function initializes a Flask web application by setting up its core configuration and infrastructure. It establishes the app's name, directories for static files and templates, and creates a command-line interface for managing the app. Most importantly, it automatically registers a route to serve static files (like CSS, JavaScript, images) from a designated folder.
__init__.technical: Calls parent class `__init__` with all configuration parameters, then creates a Click CLI group (`cli.AppGroup`). If static files are configured, it validates that `static_host` and `host_matching` are compatible, creates a weak reference to self to avoid circular references, and registers a URL rule that maps the static path to `send_static_file()` via a lambda view function. The weak reference prevents memory leaks between the app and its view function.

get_send_file_max_age.human: This function determines how long a web browser should cache a file when using Flask's `send_file` feature. It retrieves the cache duration from the application's configuration and converts it into a standard format (seconds). If no cache duration is configured, it returns `None`, which tells the browser to check for file updates instead of blindly using a cached version.
get_send_file_max_age.technical: Retrieves `SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT` from the application config. Returns `None` if the config value is `None` (no caching). If the value is a `timedelta` object, converts it to seconds via `total_seconds()` and casts to `int`. Otherwise returns the raw config value, which is expected to be an integer representing seconds.

send_static_file.human: This function serves static files (like CSS, JavaScript, images) from a designated folder when users request them. It's the mechanism that allows a web application to deliver static assets to browsers. It also validates that a static folder has been configured and calculates appropriate cache expiration times for the files.
send_static_file.technical: The function validates that `has_static_folder` is set, otherwise raises a `RuntimeError`. It retrieves the cache duration via `get_send_file_max_age(filename)`, then delegates file delivery to `send_from_directory()` with the static folder path, filename, and calculated `max_age`. Returns a Response object containing the file or appropriate HTTP headers for caching.

open_resource.human: This function allows you to safely open resource files (like configuration or data files) that are stored alongside your application code. It provides a controlled way to read these files by joining them to the app's root directory and validating that only read operations are allowed. It handles both text and binary file modes with optional encoding support.
open_resource.technical: Validates that the mode parameter is one of {"r", "rt", "rb"} and raises ValueError otherwise. Constructs the full file path by joining `self.root_path` with the relative resource path using `os.path.join()`. For binary mode ("rb"), opens the file without encoding; for text modes ("r", "rt"), opens with the specified encoding parameter. Returns an open file object ready for reading.

open_instance_resource.human: This function opens files stored in the application's instance folder, allowing both reading and writing operations. It takes a relative file path and opens it with the specified mode and encoding.
